paragraphs.text: The present proceedings emanate from a statutory reference made by the Central Government under Section 233(5) of the Companies Act, 2013, through the Regional Director, who upon consideration of the material placed on record, formed an opinion that the proposed Scheme of Amalgamation warrants examination by this Tribunal, inasmuch as the issues involved have a direct bearing on statutory compliance and public interest.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The present proceedings emanate from a statutory reference made by the Central Government under Section 233(5) of the Companies Act, 2013, through the Regional Director, who upon consideration of the material placed on record, formed an opinion that the proposed Scheme of Amalgamation warrants examination by this Tribunal, inasmuch as the issues involved have a direct bearing on statutory compliance and public interest.

paragraphs.text: The brief particulars of the proposed Scheme of Amalgamation, as given in the pleadings and documents placed on record by the parties, are as follows: All the Respondent Companies have their respective registered offices situated in the National Capital Territory of Delhi. Respondent No. 1 and Respondent No. 2, i.e., the Transferor Companies, were originally promoted by Mahindra Offgrid Services Private Limited during the financial years 2013 - 14 and 2014 - 15 respectively, in the State of Maharashtra. During the financial year 2014 - 15, Respondent/Transferor Company No. 1 entered into a Power Purchase Agreement with Southern Power Distribution Company of Telangana Limited for the establishment of a 30 MW (AC) solar power plant at Ranga Reddy District in the State of Telangana. Similarly, Respondent/Transferor Company No. 2 entered into a Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) with Northern Power Distribution Company of Telangana Limited in February 2016 for the supply of 50 MW (AC) solar power. The Transferor Companies ceased to be subsidiaries of Mahindra Offgrid Services Private Limited on 27.03.2020 and 16.04.2020, respectively, upon their acquisition by CPL India Private Limited, presently known as Apraava Energy Private Limited (Respondent No. 3/Transferee Company) Respondent No. 3/Transferee Company is jointly controlled by CLP GPEC (Mauritius) Holdings Limited, which holds 1,421,345,805 equity shares (constituting 50%) together with China Light (Mauritius) Limited holding 1 equity share as nominee, and CDPQ Infrastructures Asia II Pte. Ltd., which holds 1,421,345,806 equity shares (constituting the remaining 50%), as on 31.03.2024. VI. CLP GPEC (Mauritius) Holdings Limited and CDPQ Infrastructure Asia II Pte. Ltd. are foreign entities incorporated in Mauritius and Singapore, respectively. VII. The present Scheme of Amalgamation is proposed to take effect from the Appointed Date, i.e., 01 April 2024. VIII. Respondent Nos. 1 and 2/Transferor Companies are wholly owned subsidiaries of Respondent No. 3/Transferee Company. Upon the Scheme becoming effective, all equity shares held by the Transferee Company and its nominee in the Transferor Companies shall stand cancelled and extinguished. IX. Clause 8 of the Scheme, proposes that the Respondents may revise their financial statements for any relevant financial year, as well as income tax returns, withholding tax returns, GST returns, and other statutory filings under applicable tax laws, notwithstanding the expiry of the prescribed timelines, for the purpose of obtaining TDS certificates (including those relating to inter se transactions between the Transferor Companies and the Transferee Company), and for claiming refunds, advance tax, and withholding tax credits, as may be applicable pursuant to the Scheme. It is further provided that such reconstruction of accounts shall be deemed to have been undertaken in compliance with Section 131 of the Companies Act, 2013. X. As per Form CAA-11 dated 27.09.2024, the members and creditors of the respective Companies have accorded their consent/no objection, as required under Section 233(1)(b) and 233(1)(d) of the Companies Act, 2013.

sentences.text: Upon the Scheme becoming effective, all equity shares held by the Transferee Company and its nominee in the Transferor Companies shall stand cancelled and extinguished.

sentences.label: Issue
sentences.text: IX. Clause 8 of the Scheme, proposes that the Respondents may revise their financial statements for any relevant financial year, as well as income tax returns, withholding tax returns, GST returns, and other statutory filings under applicable tax laws, notwithstanding the expiry of the prescribed timelines, for the purpose of obtaining TDS certificates (including those relating to inter se transactions between the Transferor Companies and the Transferee Company), and for claiming refunds, advance tax, and withholding tax credits, as may be applicable pursuant to the Scheme.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: It is further provided that such reconstruction of accounts shall be deemed to have been undertaken in compliance with Section 131 of the Companies Act, 2013.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: X. As per Form CAA-11 dated 27.09.2024, the members and creditors of the respective Companies have accorded their consent/no objection, as required under Section 233(1)(b) and 233(1)(d) of the Companies Act, 2013.

paragraphs.text: The factual matrix and chronology of events, as stated by the Respondents and relevant for determination of the issues involved in the present case, reads thus: - On 03.10.2024, the Amalgamated/Transferee Company filed Form CAA-11 in e-Form RD-1 vide SRN No. AB1404887, enclosing the Scheme. A physical copy of Form CAA-11 along with the Scheme was submitted to the Petitioner on 07.10.2024 for approval. II. On 16.10.2024, Petitioner addressed an email to Amalgamated/Transferee Company forwarding representation/observations of the Registrar of Companies, Delhi, in relation to the Scheme. III. On 30.10.2024, the Amalgamated/Transferee Company submitted its response to the Petitioner dealing with the observations raised by the Registrar of Companies. IV. On 02.02.2025, upon completion of the requisite filings by the Companies, the Petitioner issued an Approval Order approving the Scheme, pursuant to which the Scheme was implemented. Consequent thereto, the Amalgamating Companies stood amalgamated with Respondent No. 1, i.e., the Amalgamated Company. V. On 05.02.2025, the Companies filed e-Form INC-28 with the Registrar of Companies, Ministry of Corporate Affairs. Subsequently, on 22.11.2025, the Companies received an email from the Ministry of Corporate Affairs intimating defects in e-Form INC-28. VI. The Ministry of Corporate Affairs directed the Amalgamated Company to furnish a certified copy of the Approval Order on or before 07.12.2025, failing which a fresh application would be required to be filed. VII. The Amalgamated Company thereafter approached the Petitioner seeking issuance of a certified copy of the Approval Order. However, no response was received from the Petitioner, nor was a certified copy supplied. VIII. Re-submission was made by the Amalgamated/Transferee Company on 01.02.2025. The Petitioner thereafter approved e-Form RD-1, and the Amalgamated/Transferee Company received the Approval Order on 02.02.2025. The Companies subsequently filed the Approval Order in e- Form INC-28 on 05.02.2025. IX. Respondent No. 1 subsequently came to know that the Petitioner had approached this Tribunal seeking rejection of the Scheme.

paragraphs.text: The Petitioner i.e., the Regional Director, while considering the Scheme of Amalgamation has raised certain objections/observations in respect of the Scheme of Amalgamation proposed between the Respondent Companies, which reads thus:- The Scheme is stated to be contrary to public interest and an inspection/investigation has ordered into affairs of Amalgamated Company by the Central Government. The said inspection was ordered on the basis of certain substantive observations, inter alia, concerning the entry of Apraava Energy Private Limited (AEPL) into new sectors such as power transmission and smart metering, and its operations in certain geographical regions including Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, and certain North-Eastern States including Assam. In this regard, the Central Government is of opinion to carry out the inspection under Section 206(5) of the Companies Act, 2013 in respect of Respondent No.

paragraphs.text: The inspection was directed in view of certain material concerns relating to the nature of operations of the Transferee Company in critical infrastructure sectors such as power transmission and smart metering, its operational presence across multiple geographically sensitive regions, and issues pertaining to beneficial ownership and regulatory compliance. In such circumstances, where inspection proceedings are stated to be pending and regulatory scrutiny is ongoing, it is contended that permitting the proposed fast-track amalgamation to attain finality without judicial examination may not be consistent with the larger public interest. Under the Scheme, the Companies propose to recast/revise their financial statements and income tax returns, which according to the Central Government, is impermissible in terms of Sections 130 and 131 of the Companies Act, 2013; Respondent Companies have not submitted the Declaration of Solvency in Form CAA-10 as required under the provisions of the Act; Certain statutory dues remain unpaid on account of disputes, and that such non-payment may entail consequences, including those contemplated under Section 240 of the Companies Act, 2013; The Auditor, in the Audit Report for the financial year ended 31.03.2023, has remarked that more than 10% of the shares of the Company are held by another body corporate and that Form BEN-2 has not been filed in this regard; and VI. The title deeds in respect of certain immovable properties are not presently in the name of the Amalgamated/Transferee Company.

paragraphs.text: The Respondent Companies have filed their replies and made submissions in response to the observations and objections raised by the Regional Director, i.e., the Petitioner, which reads thus: The Petition is liable to be dismissed at the threshold in view of the Approval Order dated 02.02.2025 issued by the Petitioner, whereby the Scheme of Amalgamation was duly accepted and approved. The Companies have filed the said Approval Order in e-Form INC-28 with the Registrar of Companies and have acted upon the Scheme by arranging their affairs in accordance therewith, rendering the present Petition infructuous. The Scheme is in public interest and does not impede or obstruct any investigation. The amalgamation pertains to wholly owned subsidiaries and their holding company, and therefore does not adversely affect any element of public interest. The Companies are private limited entities and do not involve public funds. The Scheme has been duly approved by the shareholders of the respective Companies in their Extraordinary General Meetings. The Companies are engaged in similar business activities pertaining to power generation. The Amalgamated/Transferee Company has established its creditworthiness in the market and has been assigned various contracts by different Public Sector Undertakings. The proposed amalgamation will not impede or prejudice the ongoing inspection. Pursuant to the letter dated 15 December 2023 issued by Central Government seeking certain clarifications, Amalgamated Company duly furnished its responses along with the requisite information and documents, and also complied with subsequent communications seeking additional details. It is contended that, over the past three years, no proceedings have been initiated nor any coercive action taken against the Amalgamated Company or its promoters. The Respondents therefore submits that the Scheme ought not to be rejected merely on account of a pending inspection, particularly in the absence of any adverse action, and undertakes to preserve and produce all relevant records as may be required by the Central Government. Clause 8 of the Scheme provides that the Amalgamated Company may revise its financial statements, income tax returns, GST returns and other statutory returns solely for the limited purpose of claiming refunds, advance tax and withholding tax credits, and only if so required by the concerned authorities or mandated by law. The said clause expressly clarifies that any recasting or revision of financial statements, if undertaken, shall be in strict compliance with Sections 130 and 131 of the Companies Act. It is stated that the Scheme does not contemplate any recasting of accounts contrary to the Companies Act, but only permits revision of returns in accordance with applicable taxation statutes, and the Amalgamated Company has undertaken to adhere strictly to the statutory requirements in this regard. Certain additional objections have also been raised by the Regional Director, to which the Respondent Companies have furnished their replies in a tabular form, which reads thus: Objections Response The Companies have not submitted declarations that they are solvent. The Companies had duly submitted their declarations dated 17th September 2024, affirming that they are solvent at the time of submission of the Scheme. The declarations were once again submitted with the response to objections. The Amalgamating Companies seek to absolve themselves from paying statutory dues Clause 5.5 of the Scheme stipulates that all liabilities, obligations, pending proceedings including statutory dues of the Amalgamating Companies shall be transferred to Amalgamated Company. Therefore, outstanding liability, if any, shall be paid by the Amalgamated Company. The Amalgamated Company has not submitted BEN-2 Form As per Rules 3 and 4 of the Significant Beneficial Owners Rules, pre- requisite for a company to file a BEN-2 form is that shareholders of such company shall submit a BEN-1 form. In the facts of this case, no significant shareholders of Amalgamated Company have submitted a BEN-1 form. Therefore, there was no occasion for the Amalgamated Company to submit the BEN-2 form. Immoveable properties are not in name of Amalgamated Company All immoveable properties as highlighted by the Petitioner are in the name of the Amalgamated Company as per Section 187 of the Act. Therefore, this objection is contrary to the record.

paragraphs.text: OBSERVATIONS OF THIS TRIBUNAL We have heard the Learned Counsels for the Petitioners and the Respondents and perused the averments made in the petitions, replies and the written submissions filed by the parties and have gone through the documents on records filed by the parties. The scheme has been placed before this Authority under the provisions of Section 233 of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Rule 25 of the Companies (Compromises, Arrangements and Amalgamations) Rules, 2016. Section 233 of the Companies Act, 2013 was introduced as a special provision to facilitate expeditious mergers and amalgamations between certain classes of companies, namely small companies, holding companies and their wholly-owned subsidiaries, and such other prescribed classes. The legislative intent underlying the provision is to simplify the restructuring process in cases where the likelihood of stakeholder prejudice is minimal and the corporate structure is relatively uncomplicated. Unlike comprehensive procedure contemplated under Sections 230 to 232 of the Act, which requires sanction of the Tribunal, Section 233 provides for a fast-track mechanism subject to approval of shareholders and creditors, and confirmation by the Central Government through the Regional Director. The provision represents a conscious policy shift towards ease of doing business and reduction of procedural burdens in intra group or small scale restructurings, while preserving regulatory oversight through scrutiny by statutory authorities. The provision thus balances administrative efficiency with safeguards to ensure that the interests of creditors, members, and the public are not adversely affected. Section 233 of the Act, reads thus:- "233. Merger or amalgamation of certain companies. (1) Notwithstanding the provisions of section 230 and section 232, a scheme of merger or amalgamation may be entered into between two or more small companies or between a holding company and its wholly-owned subsidiary company or such other class or classes of companies as may be prescribed, subject to the following, namely:- (a) a notice of the proposed scheme inviting objections or suggestions, if any, from the Registrar and Official Liquidators where registered office of the respective companies are situated or persons affected by the scheme within thirty days is issued by the transferor company or companies and the transferee company; (b) the objections and suggestions received are considered by the companies in their respective general meetings and the scheme is approved by the respective members or class of members at a general meeting holding at least ninety percent of the total number of shares; (c) each of the companies involved in the merger files a declaration of solvency, in the prescribed form, with the Registrar of the place where the registered office of the company is situated; and (d) the scheme is approved by majority representing nine-tenths in value of the creditors or class of creditors of respective companies indicated in a meeting convened by the company by giving a notice of twenty-one days along with the scheme to its creditors for the purpose or otherwise approved in writing. (2) The transferee company shall file a copy of the scheme so approved in the manner as may be prescribed, with the Central Government, Registrar and the Official Liquidator where the registered office of the company is situated. (3) On the receipt of the scheme, if the Registrar or the Official Liquidator has no objections or suggestions to the scheme, the Central Government shall register the same and issue a confirmation thereof to the companies. (4) If the Registrar or Official Liquidator has any objections or suggestions, he may communicate the same in writing to the Central Government within a period of thirty days: Provided that if no such communication is made, it shall be presumed that he has no objection to the scheme. (5) If the Central Government after receiving the objections or suggestions or for any reason is of the opinion that such a scheme is not in public interest or in the interest of the creditors, it may file an application before the Tribunal within a period of sixty days of the receipt of the scheme under sub-section (2) stating its objections and requesting that the Tribunal may consider the scheme under section 232. (6) On receipt of an application from the Central Government or from any person, if the Tribunal, for reasons to be recorded in writing, is of the opinion that the scheme should be considered as per the procedure laid down in section 232, the Tribunal may direct accordingly or it may confirm the scheme by passing such order as it deems fit: Provided that if the Central Government does not have any objection to the scheme or it does not file any application under this section before the Tribunal, it shall be deemed that it has no objection to the scheme. (7) A copy of the order under sub-section (6) confirming the scheme shall be communicated to the Registrar having jurisdiction over the transferee company and the persons concerned and the Registrar shall register the scheme and issue a confirmation thereof to the companies and such confirmation shall be communicated to the Registrars where transferor company or companies were situated. (8) The registration of the scheme under sub-section (3) or sub- section (7) shall be deemed to have the effect of dissolution of the transferor company without process of winding-up. (9) The registration of the scheme shall have the following effects, namely:- (a) transfer of property or liabilities of the transferor company to the transferee company so that the property becomes the property of the transferee company and the liabilities become the liabilities of the transferee company; (b) the charges, if any, on the property of the transferor company shall be applicable and enforceable as if the charges were on the property of the transferee company; (c) legal proceedings by or against the transferor company pending before any court of law shall be continued by or against the transferee company; and (d) where the scheme provides for purchase of shares held by the dissenting shareholders or settlement of debt due to dissenting creditors, such amount, to the extent it is unpaid, shall become the liability of the transferee company. (10) A transferee company shall not on merger or amalgamation, hold any shares in its own name or in the name of any trust either on its behalf or on behalf of any of its subsidiary or associate company and all such shares shall be cancelled or extinguished on the merger or amalgamation. (11) The transferee company shall file an application with the Registrar along with the scheme registered, indicating the revised authorised capital and pay the prescribed fees due on revised capital: Provided that the fee, if any, paid by the transferor company on its authorised capital prior to its merger or amalgamation with the transferee company shall be set-off against the fees payable by the transferee company on its authorised capital enhanced by the merger or amalgamation. (12) The provisions of this section shall mutatis mutandis apply to a company or companies specified in sub-section (1) in respect of a scheme of compromise or arrangement referred to in section 230 or division or transfer of a company referred to clause (b) of sub-section (1) of section 232. (13) The Central Government may provide for the merger or amalgamation of companies in such manner as may be prescribed. (14) A company covered under this section may use the provisions of section 232 for the approval of any scheme for merger or amalgamation." As can be seen from the petition, it is the contention of the Regional Director in the petition that the Scheme is not in the interest of the public. In this regard, it becomes necessary to examine the scope and meaning of the expression "public interest." The expression "public interest" is not specifically defined under the Companies Act, 2013, however, it embodies the broader concept of safeguarding the collective welfare and protecting interests in which the public at large has a stake, particularly such interests as warrant regulatory oversight. In company law jurisprudence, the concept of public interest assumes significance in ensuring that the rights of all stakeholders are duly protected. This legislative intent is reflected, in Section 233(1) of the Act, in which notice of the proposed Scheme is required to be issued to the Registrar of Companies and the Official Liquidator; objections and suggestions are invited from members and creditors; and approval of members holding at least 90% of the total number of shares, or creditors representing 90% in value, is mandated. These statutory requirements reflect the legislative intent to balance corporate restructuring with the protection of stakeholders and the larger public interest. In this context, reference may be made to the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in 63 Moons Technologies Ltd. (formerly known as Financial Technologies India Ltd.) v. Union of India & Ors., (2019) 18 SCC 401, wherein the scope and ambit of the expression "public interest" was discussed. The relevant excerpt of the judgement reads thus: "In the context of compulsory amalgamation of two or more companies, the expression "public interest" would mean the welfare of the public or the interest of society as a whole, as contrasted with the "selfish" interest of a group of private individuals. Thus, "public interest" may have regard to the interest of production of goods or services essential to the nation so that they may contribute to the nation's welfare and progress, and in so doing, may also provide much needed employment. "Public interest" in this context would, therefore, mean the combining of resources of two or more companies so as to impact production and consumption of goods and services and employment of persons relatable thereto for the general benefit of the community. Conversely, any action that impedes promotion of industry or obstructs growth which is in national or public interest would run counter to public interest as mentioned in this Section." In the present case, the Regional Director has contended that Apraava Energy Private Limited (AEPL), having diversified into sectors such as power transmission and smart metering and having operations in certain sensitive geographical locations including Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Telangana and certain States in the North-East including Assam, may pose a potential risk. However, upon being queried as to how such diversification or geographical presence would adversely impact the proposed Scheme of Merger and Amalgamation, the learned counsel for the Regional Director was unable to furnish any cogent or satisfactory explanation to substantiate the said contention. We are unable to appreciate how diversification of business activities in different locations, in the absence of any illegality or violation of statutory provisions, could operate as a bar to a proposed amalgamation. Diversification through merger and amalgamation is a legitimate commercial strategy aimed at managing business cycles, reducing dependency on a single market and achieving financial and managerial efficiencies. In such restructuring, entities engaged in different or diversified sectors or at different locations combine to create a broader and less risk-dependent business portfolio. Besides, the transferor companies are subsidiary of transferee company and whether they are amalgamated or not, their operation of area would remain the same. Once, the Transferee Company is currently operating in particular geographical zone, it is not understood that how the amalgamation of Transferor Companies in their holding company would make a difference to it. In the absence of any specific illegality or statutory infraction, prejudice to stakeholders or contravention of law, the objection raised by the Regional Director on this account is found bereft of merits and cannot be countenanced. The Regional Director has also raised the issue of inspection under Section 206(5) of the Companies Act, 2013 being carried out by the Central Government in respect of Respondent No. 3 and that the same is at an advance stage. It has, however, been undertaken by the Amalgamated Company that all records of the Amalgamating Entities shall be duly preserved and made available for the purposes of such inspection. Considering that Scheme contemplates amalgamation of wholly owned subsidiaries with their parent company, no prejudice, per se, would be caused to the ongoing inspection. Once, even after amalgamation, the Respondent No. 3 viz. Transferee Company would be in existence, we are unable to appreciate that in what manner, the amalgamation of subsidiary companies in their holding company would adversely impact the aforementioned inspection. In our view, the apprehension of the Regional Director can be adequately addressed by directing that, for the purposes of any investigation, inspection or other proceedings, the Transferee Company shall ensure that the amalgamation does not impede such process and shall maintain and produce all relevant records reflecting the pre- amalgamation position. It is further clarified that, if so required for the purposes of investigation or inspection or proceedings, the Transferor Company shall be deemed to continue to exist to facilitate such investigation or inspection or proceedings. Thus, it is made clear that any pending proceedings before any statutory or regulatory authority shall remain unaffected by the approval of the Scheme of Amalgamation and shall continue in accordance with law, without any prejudice. Any liability that may arise pursuant to such proceedings, including those crystallising after the Effective Date, shall be borne and discharged by the Transferee Company. The Transferee Company shall extend full cooperation and assistance to the concerned authorities, and this order shall not be construed as impeding or prejudicing any investigation, inspection, or other proceedings. VI. According to RD the Respondent Companies propose to recast or revise their financial statements and income tax returns, which, according to the Central Government, would be impermissible under Sections 130 and 131 of the Companies Act, 2013. In response, the Respondent Companies have submitted that Clause 8 of the Scheme permits revision of financial statements, income tax returns, GST returns and other statutory returns solely for the limited purpose of claiming refunds, advance tax and withholding tax credits, and only where so required by the competent authorities or mandated by law. The said clause further clarifies that any reconstruction or revision of financial documents shall be undertaken strictly in compliance with Sections 130 and 131 of the Act. We are not oblivious of the fact that in schemes of merger and amalgamation sanctioned by this Tribunal, consequential alignment of financial statements of the Transferor and Transferee Companies may be required in accordance with the applicable Accounting Standards, including AS-14 or Ind AS-103, as the case may be. Insofar as the apprehension of the Regional Director regarding possible manipulation or fraudulent recasting of accounts is concerned, it is clarified that any revision of financial statements, income tax returns, GST returns or other statutory filings shall be carried out strictly in accordance with law, without contravening Sections 130 or 131 of the Act or any other statutory provision, and without causing prejudice to the interests of stakeholders. It is further clarified that if any party is aggrieved by any such revision or action undertaken pursuant to the Scheme in violation of law, it shall be at liberty to seek appropriate remedies in accordance with law against the Transferee Company. VII. Insofar as statutory dues are concerned, it has been submitted by the Transferee Company and Transferor Company No. 2 that no statutory demands are presently outstanding against them. However, in respect of Transferor Company No. 1, it is noted that certain income tax demands pertaining to the Financial Years 2015 - 16 and 2016 - 17 are pending adjudication in appeal. The details thereof, reads thus: VIII. In the wake, it is made clear that the rights of the Income Tax Department shall not, in any manner, be affected by the sanction of the present Scheme. It is further directed that the outcome of the pending appeals shall be binding, and in the event any demand arises in respect of the Transferor Companies, the same shall be duly borne and discharged by the Transferee Company. Additionally, any proceedings initiated or continued by the Income Tax Department against the Transferor Companies shall, upon the Scheme becoming effective, be deemed to have been initiated or continued against the Transferee Company, which shall be liable to respond and comply in accordance with law. The Transferee Company, which shall be deemed to step into the shoes of the Transferor Companies for such purposes. IX. Another objection raised by the Petitioner is that the Declaration of Solvency has not been placed on record by the Respondent Companies. In response, the Respondent Companies have submitted that the Declaration of Solvency has been duly filed as Annexure R-8 along with their reply and that the same had already been furnished to the Petitioner. In this regard, it is pertinent to make reference to the judgment of the Hon'ble Bombay High Court in Asset Auto India Pvt. Ltd. and Ors. v. Union of India and Ors., (2024) ibclaw.in 720 HC, in which it was held that:- "the declaration of solvency has been filed. The scheme has also been approved as required by sub-section (1) of Section 233. The said section does not empower respondent no.2 to reject the declaration filed but if at all respondent no.2 is of the opinion that any of the conditions is not satisfied then appropriate application has to be made to the Tribunal within the prescribed period objecting to the scheme." In view of the aforesaid, we are of view that the Regional Director may take the Declaration of Solvency on record, and in the event of any dissatisfaction regarding compliance with statutory requirements, it would be open to the Regional Director to approach the Tribunal within the prescribed period in accordance with law. X. The Respondent Companies have furnished replies to all other observations raised by the Registrar of Companies/Regional Director. Upon consideration of the same, we are satisfied with the explanations offered by the Respondent Companies. However, we make it clear that the approval of the Scheme shall not absolve the Respondent Companies from strict compliance with the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013 and the rules, regulations framed thereunder, as well as any other applicable laws for the time being in force. XI. It is made also clear that if at any stage the undertakings/commitments made qua the observation made by the RD are not satisfied or flawed, the present order would become non est. It is further directed that any term contrary to the provisions of Section 6 of Companies Act, 2016 contained in the scheme would be non est. XII. This tribunal hereby directs that any liability, whether existing or arising hereafter, pertaining to the Transferor companies, shall be borne by the Transferee Company after the amalgamation. XIII. The present order, it would not affect any of the pending investigation/ proceedings/ inquiry qua any of the applicants and/ or their promoters/ directors/KMPs/ any other staff associated with the affairs of the companies. XIV. While approving the Scheme as above, it is clarified that this Order should not be construed as an order in any way granting exemption from payment of Stamp Duty, Taxes or other statutory dues, if any, and payment in accordance with law or in respect to any permission/compliance with any other requirement, which may be specifically required under any law. Further the approval of the Scheme would in no manner affect the tax treatment of the transactions under the Income Tax Act, 1961 or serve as any exemption or defence for the Respondent Companies against tax treatment in accordance with the provisions of Income Tax Act, 1961 and the rules and regulations made thereunder. XV. The approval of scheme would not expand the geographical area / zone which is already covered by operation of business of Transferor and Transferee Company. The present order would not absolve the amalgamated company from seeking any such approval from Central, Government, any Authority regulating the affairs at, National, State, District or local level, which currently the Transferor Companies or Transferee Company are under obligation to obtain. XVI. The amalgamated company would not be entitled to any refund or advance tax, withholding of tax credit or immunity from demand of income tax. XVII. As the Transferor Company are subsidiary of Transferee Company, the approval of present schemes would not hinder the action in progress under Section 206(6) of the Companies Act, 2013. If, for any reason, to carry out the process as per Section 206(6), the Central Government would meet to treat the Transferor Companies in existence as they are existing now, for the purpose of such process, all the three companies would be deemed in existence, as they are existing now and the amalgamation would only in terms of the scheme for which approval is sought. XVIII. Any of the provisions of the scheme which is repugnant to the provisions of Companies Act, 2013 or is hit by the provisions of Section 6(b) thereof would be void and non-est.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: OBSERVATIONS OF THIS TRIBUNAL We have heard the Learned Counsels for the Petitioners and the Respondents and perused the averments made in the petitions, replies and the written submissions filed by the parties and have gone through the documents on records filed by the parties.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The scheme has been placed before this Authority under the provisions of Section 233 of the Companies Act, 2013 read with Rule 25 of the Companies (Compromises, Arrangements and Amalgamations) Rules, 2016.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Section 233 of the Companies Act, 2013 was introduced as a special provision to facilitate expeditious mergers and amalgamations between certain classes of companies, namely small companies, holding companies and their wholly-owned subsidiaries, and such other prescribed classes.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The legislative intent underlying the provision is to simplify the restructuring process in cases where the likelihood of stakeholder prejudice is minimal and the corporate structure is relatively uncomplicated.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Unlike comprehensive procedure contemplated under Sections 230 to 232 of the Act, which requires sanction of the Tribunal, Section 233 provides for a fast-track mechanism subject to approval of shareholders and creditors, and confirmation by the Central Government through the Regional Director.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The provision represents a conscious policy shift towards ease of doing business and reduction of procedural burdens in intra group or small scale restructurings, while preserving regulatory oversight through scrutiny by statutory authorities.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The provision thus balances administrative efficiency with safeguards to ensure that the interests of creditors, members, and the public are not adversely affected.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Section 233 of the Act, reads thus:- "233.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Merger or amalgamation of certain companies.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (1) Notwithstanding the provisions of section 230 and section 232, a scheme of merger or amalgamation may be entered into between two or more small companies or between a holding company and its wholly-owned subsidiary company or such other class or classes of companies as may be prescribed, subject to the following, namely:- (a) a notice of the proposed scheme inviting objections or suggestions, if any, from the Registrar and Official Liquidators where registered office of the respective companies are situated or persons affected by the scheme within thirty days is issued by the transferor company or companies and the transferee company; (b) the objections and suggestions received are considered by the companies in their respective general meetings and the scheme is approved by the respective members or class of members at a general meeting holding at least ninety percent of the total number of shares; (c) each of the companies involved in the merger files a declaration of solvency, in the prescribed form, with the Registrar of the place where the registered office of the company is situated; and (d) the scheme is approved by majority representing nine-tenths in value of the creditors or class of creditors of respective companies indicated in a meeting convened by the company by giving a notice of twenty-one days along with the scheme to its creditors for the purpose or otherwise approved in writing.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (2) The transferee company shall file a copy of the scheme so approved in the manner as may be prescribed, with the Central Government, Registrar and the Official Liquidator where the registered office of the company is situated.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (3) On the receipt of the scheme, if the Registrar or the Official Liquidator has no objections or suggestions to the scheme, the Central Government shall register the same and issue a confirmation thereof to the companies.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (4) If the Registrar or Official Liquidator has any objections or suggestions, he may communicate the same in writing to the Central Government within a period of thirty days: Provided that if no such communication is made, it shall be presumed that he has no objection to the scheme.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (5) If the Central Government after receiving the objections or suggestions or for any reason is of the opinion that such a scheme is not in public interest or in the interest of the creditors, it may file an application before the Tribunal within a period of sixty days of the receipt of the scheme under sub-section (2) stating its objections and requesting that the Tribunal may consider the scheme under section 232.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (6) On receipt of an application from the Central Government or from any person, if the Tribunal, for reasons to be recorded in writing, is of the opinion that the scheme should be considered as per the procedure laid down in section 232, the Tribunal may direct accordingly or it may confirm the scheme by passing such order as it deems fit: Provided that if the Central Government does not have any objection to the scheme or it does not file any application under this section before the Tribunal, it shall be deemed that it has no objection to the scheme.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (7) A copy of the order under sub-section (6) confirming the scheme shall be communicated to the Registrar having jurisdiction over the transferee company and the persons concerned and the Registrar shall register the scheme and issue a confirmation thereof to the companies and such confirmation shall be communicated to the Registrars where transferor company or companies were situated.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (8) The registration of the scheme under sub-section (3) or sub- section (7) shall be deemed to have the effect of dissolution of the transferor company without process of winding-up.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (9) The registration of the scheme shall have the following effects, namely:- (a) transfer of property or liabilities of the transferor company to the transferee company so that the property becomes the property of the transferee company and the liabilities become the liabilities of the transferee company; (b) the charges, if any, on the property of the transferor company shall be applicable and enforceable as if the charges were on the property of the transferee company; (c) legal proceedings by or against the transferor company pending before any court of law shall be continued by or against the transferee company; and (d) where the scheme provides for purchase of shares held by the dissenting shareholders or settlement of debt due to dissenting creditors, such amount, to the extent it is unpaid, shall become the liability of the transferee company.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (10) A transferee company shall not on merger or amalgamation, hold any shares in its own name or in the name of any trust either on its behalf or on behalf of any of its subsidiary or associate company and all such shares shall be cancelled or extinguished on the merger or amalgamation.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (11) The transferee company shall file an application with the Registrar along with the scheme registered, indicating the revised authorised capital and pay the prescribed fees due on revised capital: Provided that the fee, if any, paid by the transferor company on its authorised capital prior to its merger or amalgamation with the transferee company shall be set-off against the fees payable by the transferee company on its authorised capital enhanced by the merger or amalgamation.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (12) The provisions of this section shall mutatis mutandis apply to a company or companies specified in sub-section (1) in respect of a scheme of compromise or arrangement referred to in section 230 or division or transfer of a company referred to clause (b) of sub-section (1) of section 232.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: (13) The Central Government may provide for the merger or amalgamation of companies in such manner as may be prescribed.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: (14) A company covered under this section may use the provisions of section 232 for the approval of any scheme for merger or amalgamation." As can be seen from the petition, it is the contention of the Regional Director in the petition that the Scheme is not in the interest of the public.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: In this regard, it becomes necessary to examine the scope and meaning of the expression "public interest." The expression "public interest" is not specifically defined under the Companies Act, 2013, however, it embodies the broader concept of safeguarding the collective welfare and protecting interests in which the public at large has a stake, particularly such interests as warrant regulatory oversight.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: In company law jurisprudence, the concept of public interest assumes significance in ensuring that the rights of all stakeholders are duly protected.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: This legislative intent is reflected, in Section 233(1) of the Act, in which notice of the proposed Scheme is required to be issued to the Registrar of Companies and the Official Liquidator; objections and suggestions are invited from members and creditors; and approval of members holding at least 90% of the total number of shares, or creditors representing 90% in value, is mandated.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: These statutory requirements reflect the legislative intent to balance corporate restructuring with the protection of stakeholders and the larger public interest.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: In this context, reference may be made to the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in 63 Moons Technologies Ltd. (formerly known as Financial Technologies India Ltd.) v. Union of India & Ors., (2019) 18 SCC 401, wherein the scope and ambit of the expression "public interest" was discussed.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The relevant excerpt of the judgement reads thus: "In the context of compulsory amalgamation of two or more companies, the expression "public interest" would mean the welfare of the public or the interest of society as a whole, as contrasted with the "selfish" interest of a group of private individuals.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Thus, "public interest" may have regard to the interest of production of goods or services essential to the nation so that they may contribute to the nation's welfare and progress, and in so doing, may also provide much needed employment.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: "Public interest" in this context would, therefore, mean the combining of resources of two or more companies so as to impact production and consumption of goods and services and employment of persons relatable thereto for the general benefit of the community.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: Conversely, any action that impedes promotion of industry or obstructs growth which is in national or public interest would run counter to public interest as mentioned in this Section." In the present case, the Regional Director has contended that Apraava Energy Private Limited (AEPL), having diversified into sectors such as power transmission and smart metering and having operations in certain sensitive geographical locations including Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Telangana and certain States in the North-East including Assam, may pose a potential risk.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: However, upon being queried as to how such diversification or geographical presence would adversely impact the proposed Scheme of Merger and Amalgamation, the learned counsel for the Regional Director was unable to furnish any cogent or satisfactory explanation to substantiate the said contention.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: We are unable to appreciate how diversification of business activities in different locations, in the absence of any illegality or violation of statutory provisions, could operate as a bar to a proposed amalgamation.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Diversification through merger and amalgamation is a legitimate commercial strategy aimed at managing business cycles, reducing dependency on a single market and achieving financial and managerial efficiencies.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: In such restructuring, entities engaged in different or diversified sectors or at different locations combine to create a broader and less risk-dependent business portfolio.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Besides, the transferor companies are subsidiary of transferee company and whether they are amalgamated or not, their operation of area would remain the same.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Once, the Transferee Company is currently operating in particular geographical zone, it is not understood that how the amalgamation of Transferor Companies in their holding company would make a difference to it.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: In the absence of any specific illegality or statutory infraction, prejudice to stakeholders or contravention of law, the objection raised by the Regional Director on this account is found bereft of merits and cannot be countenanced.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The Regional Director has also raised the issue of inspection under Section 206(5) of the Companies Act, 2013 being carried out by the Central Government in respect of Respondent No. 3 and that the same is at an advance stage.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: It has, however, been undertaken by the Amalgamated Company that all records of the Amalgamating Entities shall be duly preserved and made available for the purposes of such inspection.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: Considering that Scheme contemplates amalgamation of wholly owned subsidiaries with their parent company, no prejudice, per se, would be caused to the ongoing inspection.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Once, even after amalgamation, the Respondent No. 3 viz. Transferee Company would be in existence, we are unable to appreciate that in what manner, the amalgamation of subsidiary companies in their holding company would adversely impact the aforementioned inspection.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: In our view, the apprehension of the Regional Director can be adequately addressed by directing that, for the purposes of any investigation, inspection or other proceedings, the Transferee Company shall ensure that the amalgamation does not impede such process and shall maintain and produce all relevant records reflecting the pre- amalgamation position.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: It is further clarified that, if so required for the purposes of investigation or inspection or proceedings, the Transferor Company shall be deemed to continue to exist to facilitate such investigation or inspection or proceedings.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: Thus, it is made clear that any pending proceedings before any statutory or regulatory authority shall remain unaffected by the approval of the Scheme of Amalgamation and shall continue in accordance with law, without any prejudice.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Any liability that may arise pursuant to such proceedings, including those crystallising after the Effective Date, shall be borne and discharged by the Transferee Company.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The Transferee Company shall extend full cooperation and assistance to the concerned authorities, and this order shall not be construed as impeding or prejudicing any investigation, inspection, or other proceedings.

sentences.label: Reasoning
sentences.text: VI. According to RD the Respondent Companies propose to recast or revise their financial statements and income tax returns, which, according to the Central Government, would be impermissible under Sections 130 and 131 of the Companies Act, 2013.

sentences.label: Reasoning
sentences.text: In response, the Respondent Companies have submitted that Clause 8 of the Scheme permits revision of financial statements, income tax returns, GST returns and other statutory returns solely for the limited purpose of claiming refunds, advance tax and withholding tax credits, and only where so required by the competent authorities or mandated by law.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The said clause further clarifies that any reconstruction or revision of financial documents shall be undertaken strictly in compliance with Sections 130 and 131 of the Act.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: We are not oblivious of the fact that in schemes of merger and amalgamation sanctioned by this Tribunal, consequential alignment of financial statements of the Transferor and Transferee Companies may be required in accordance with the applicable Accounting Standards, including AS-14 or Ind AS-103, as the case may be.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Insofar as the apprehension of the Regional Director regarding possible manipulation or fraudulent recasting of accounts is concerned, it is clarified that any revision of financial statements, income tax returns, GST returns or other statutory filings shall be carried out strictly in accordance with law, without contravening Sections 130 or 131 of the Act or any other statutory provision, and without causing prejudice to the interests of stakeholders.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: It is further clarified that if any party is aggrieved by any such revision or action undertaken pursuant to the Scheme in violation of law, it shall be at liberty to seek appropriate remedies in accordance with law against the Transferee Company.

sentences.label: Reasoning
sentences.text: VII. Insofar as statutory dues are concerned, it has been submitted by the Transferee Company and Transferor Company No. 2 that no statutory demands are presently outstanding against them.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: However, in respect of Transferor Company No. 1, it is noted that certain income tax demands pertaining to the Financial Years 2015 - 16 and 2016 - 17 are pending adjudication in appeal.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The details thereof, reads thus: VIII. In the wake, it is made clear that the rights of the Income Tax Department shall not, in any manner, be affected by the sanction of the present Scheme.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: It is further directed that the outcome of the pending appeals shall be binding, and in the event any demand arises in respect of the Transferor Companies, the same shall be duly borne and discharged by the Transferee Company.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Additionally, any proceedings initiated or continued by the Income Tax Department against the Transferor Companies shall, upon the Scheme becoming effective, be deemed to have been initiated or continued against the Transferee Company, which shall be liable to respond and comply in accordance with law.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The Transferee Company, which shall be deemed to step into the shoes of the Transferor Companies for such purposes.

sentences.label: Argument of Respondent
sentences.text: IX. Another objection raised by the Petitioner is that the Declaration of Solvency has not been placed on record by the Respondent Companies.

sentences.label: Reasoning
sentences.text: In response, the Respondent Companies have submitted that the Declaration of Solvency has been duly filed as Annexure R-8 along with their reply and that the same had already been furnished to the Petitioner.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: In this regard, it is pertinent to make reference to the judgment of the Hon'ble Bombay High Court in Asset Auto India Pvt. Ltd. and Ors. v. Union of India and Ors., (2024) ibclaw.in 720 HC, in which it was held that:- "the declaration of solvency has been filed.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The scheme has also been approved as required by sub-section (1) of Section 233.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: The said section does not empower respondent no.2 to reject the declaration filed but if at all respondent no.2 is of the opinion that any of the conditions is not satisfied then appropriate application has to be made to the Tribunal within the prescribed period objecting to the scheme." In view of the aforesaid, we are of view that the Regional Director may take the Declaration of Solvency on record, and in the event of any dissatisfaction regarding compliance with statutory requirements, it would be open to the Regional Director to approach the Tribunal within the prescribed period in accordance with law.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: X. The Respondent Companies have furnished replies to all other observations raised by the Registrar of Companies/Regional Director.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Upon consideration of the same, we are satisfied with the explanations offered by the Respondent Companies.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: However, we make it clear that the approval of the Scheme shall not absolve the Respondent Companies from strict compliance with the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013 and the rules, regulations framed thereunder, as well as any other applicable laws for the time being in force.

sentences.label: Decision
sentences.text: XI. It is made also clear that if at any stage the undertakings/commitments made qua the observation made by the RD are not satisfied or flawed, the present order would become non est.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: It is further directed that any term contrary to the provisions of Section 6 of Companies Act, 2016 contained in the scheme would be non est.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XII. This tribunal hereby directs that any liability, whether existing or arising hereafter, pertaining to the Transferor companies, shall be borne by the Transferee Company after the amalgamation.

sentences.label: None
sentences.text: XIII. The present order, it would not affect any of the pending investigation/ proceedings/ inquiry qua any of the applicants and/ or their promoters/ directors/KMPs/ any other staff associated with the affairs of the companies.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XIV. While approving the Scheme as above, it is clarified that this Order should not be construed as an order in any way granting exemption from payment of Stamp Duty, Taxes or other statutory dues, if any, and payment in accordance with law or in respect to any permission/compliance with any other requirement, which may be specifically required under any law.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: Further the approval of the Scheme would in no manner affect the tax treatment of the transactions under the Income Tax Act, 1961 or serve as any exemption or defence for the Respondent Companies against tax treatment in accordance with the provisions of Income Tax Act, 1961 and the rules and regulations made thereunder.

sentences.label: Argument of Respondent
sentences.text: XV. The approval of scheme would not expand the geographical area / zone which is already covered by operation of business of Transferor and Transferee Company.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The present order would not absolve the amalgamated company from seeking any such approval from Central, Government, any Authority regulating the affairs at, National, State, District or local level, which currently the Transferor Companies or Transferee Company are under obligation to obtain.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XVI. The amalgamated company would not be entitled to any refund or advance tax, withholding of tax credit or immunity from demand of income tax.

sentences.label: Argument of Respondent
sentences.text: XVII. As the Transferor Company are subsidiary of Transferee Company, the approval of present schemes would not hinder the action in progress under Section 206(6) of the Companies Act, 2013.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: If, for any reason, to carry out the process as per Section 206(6), the Central Government would meet to treat the Transferor Companies in existence as they are existing now, for the purpose of such process, all the three companies would be deemed in existence, as they are existing now and the amalgamation would only in terms of the scheme for which approval is sought.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XVIII. Any of the provisions of the scheme which is repugnant to the provisions of Companies Act, 2013 or is hit by the provisions of Section 6(b) thereof would be void and non-est.

paragraphs.text: ADDITIONALLY, we issue following directions qua the Petitioner Companies:- The scheme shall be effective from the appointed date i.e., 01.04.2024. the Transferor Companies shall stand dissolved without undergoing the process of winding up; The Transferee Company shall file the revised Memorandum and Articles of Association with the concerned RoC and further make the requisite payments of the differential fee (if any) for the enhancement of authorized capital of the Transferee Company; after setting off the fees paid by the Transferor Company; The Transferee Company will clear all the pending statutory dues after exercising the option of appellate jurisdictions and as per final orders. The Scheme shall not come in the way of the statutory authorities to recover any of their dues. All the contentions of the parties shall remain open before the relevant forum(s), where disputes are pending; All benefits, entitlements, incentives and concessions under incentive schemes and policies that Transferor Companies are entitled, including under Customs, Excise, Service Tax, VAT, Sales Tax, GST and Entry Tax and Income Tax laws, subsidy receivables from Government, grant from any governmental authorities, direct tax benefit/exemptions/ deductions, shall, to the extent statutorily available and along with associated obligations, would stand transferred to and be available to the Transferee Company, as if the Transferee Company was originally entitled to all such benefits, entitlements, incentives and concessions; All the property, rights and entitlement of the Transferor Companies pertaining to the business be transferred, without further act or deed, to the Transferee Company and accordingly the same shall pursuant to Sections 230 to 232 of the Companies Act, 2013, be transferred to and vested in the Transferee Company for all the estate and interest of the Companies but subject nevertheless to all charges now affecting the same; VI. All contracts, deeds, bonds, agreements and other instruments of whatsoever nature subsisting or having effect immediately before the Effective Date and to which the Transferor Companies are parties or beneficiaries shall, without any further act or deed, stand transferred to and vested in the Transferee Company and shall remain in full force and effect against or in favour of the Transferee Company, and may be enforced by or against it as fully and effectually as if the Transferee Company had originally been a party thereto in place of the Transferor Companies. VII. All the employees of the Transferor Companies, shall be deemed to have become the employees and the staff of the Transferee Company with effect from the Appointed Date, and shall stand transferred to the Transferee Company without any interruption of service and on the terms and conditions no less favorable than those on which they are engaged by the Transferor Companies, as on the Effective Date, including in relation to the level of remuneration and contractual and statutory benefits, incentive plans, terminal benefits, gratuity plans, provident plans and any other retirement benefits; VIII. All liabilities of the Transferor Companies shall pursuant to the provisions of section 232(4) and other applicable provisions of the Company Act, 2013, to the extent they are outstanding as on the Effective Date, without any further act, instrument or deed stand transferred to and be deemed to be the debts, liabilities, contingent liabilities, duties and obligations etc. as the case may be, of the Transferee Company and shall be exercised by or against the Transferee Company in accordance with their liability, as if it had incurred such liabilities. IX. All proceedings now pending by or against the Transferor Companies be continued by or against the Transferee Company. X. The Income Tax Department shall have the liberty to determine the tax implications arising from the Scheme of amalgamation under the provisions of the Income Tax Act, and such determination shall prevail over the terms of the Scheme. In the event of any tax liabilities arising in respect of the Transferor Companies, the Transferee Company shall be responsible and liable for the same. The Income Tax Department retains the authority to pursue its investigation or inquiry or scrutiny, as deemed necessary even after the amalgamation among the Respondent companies. XI. The Transferee Company is directed to comply with the provisions of Section 233(11) of the Companies Act, 2013, in respect of payment of the requisite fees, if any, consequent upon the revision of its authorised share capital. XII. That any person interested shall be at liberty to apply to this Tribunal in the above matter for any directions that may be necessary. XIII. It is hereby clarified that the present Order shall not entitle the Transferee Company to any exemption from the requirement of obtaining any license/ permit/ registration/ quota/ clearance/ concession or grant that may be required by them from the Central Government/State Government/ Local Authority/Sectoral Regulator, or any other authority constituted under any law for the time being in force. XIV. The Respondent Companies shall, within 30 days after the date of receipt of this order, cause a certified copy of this order to be delivered to the Registrar of Companies in Form INC 28.

sentences.label: None
sentences.text: ADDITIONALLY, we issue following directions qua the Petitioner Companies:- The scheme shall be effective from the appointed date i.e., 01.04.2024. the Transferor Companies shall stand dissolved without undergoing the process of winding up; The Transferee Company shall file the revised Memorandum and Articles of Association with the concerned RoC and further make the requisite payments of the differential fee (if any) for the enhancement of authorized capital of the Transferee Company; after setting off the fees paid by the Transferor Company; The Transferee Company will clear all the pending statutory dues after exercising the option of appellate jurisdictions and as per final orders.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The Scheme shall not come in the way of the statutory authorities to recover any of their dues.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: All the contentions of the parties shall remain open before the relevant forum(s), where disputes are pending; All benefits, entitlements, incentives and concessions under incentive schemes and policies that Transferor Companies are entitled, including under Customs, Excise, Service Tax, VAT, Sales Tax, GST and Entry Tax and Income Tax laws, subsidy receivables from Government, grant from any governmental authorities, direct tax benefit/exemptions/ deductions, shall, to the extent statutorily available and along with associated obligations, would stand transferred to and be available to the Transferee Company, as if the Transferee Company was originally entitled to all such benefits, entitlements, incentives and concessions; All the property, rights and entitlement of the Transferor Companies pertaining to the business be transferred, without further act or deed, to the Transferee Company and accordingly the same shall pursuant to Sections 230 to 232 of the Companies Act, 2013, be transferred to and vested in the Transferee Company for all the estate and interest of the Companies but subject nevertheless to all charges now affecting the same; VI. All contracts, deeds, bonds, agreements and other instruments of whatsoever nature subsisting or having effect immediately before the Effective Date and to which the Transferor Companies are parties or beneficiaries shall, without any further act or deed, stand transferred to and vested in the Transferee Company and shall remain in full force and effect against or in favour of the Transferee Company, and may be enforced by or against it as fully and effectually as if the Transferee Company had originally been a party thereto in place of the Transferor Companies.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: VII. All the employees of the Transferor Companies, shall be deemed to have become the employees and the staff of the Transferee Company with effect from the Appointed Date, and shall stand transferred to the Transferee Company without any interruption of service and on the terms and conditions no less favorable than those on which they are engaged by the Transferor Companies, as on the Effective Date, including in relation to the level of remuneration and contractual and statutory benefits, incentive plans, terminal benefits, gratuity plans, provident plans and any other retirement benefits; VIII. All liabilities of the Transferor Companies shall pursuant to the provisions of section 232(4) and other applicable provisions of the Company Act, 2013, to the extent they are outstanding as on the Effective Date, without any further act, instrument or deed stand transferred to and be deemed to be the debts, liabilities, contingent liabilities, duties and obligations etc. as the case may be, of the Transferee Company and shall be exercised by or against the Transferee Company in accordance with their liability, as if it had incurred such liabilities.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: IX. All proceedings now pending by or against the Transferor Companies be continued by or against the Transferee Company.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: X. The Income Tax Department shall have the liberty to determine the tax implications arising from the Scheme of amalgamation under the provisions of the Income Tax Act, and such determination shall prevail over the terms of the Scheme.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: In the event of any tax liabilities arising in respect of the Transferor Companies, the Transferee Company shall be responsible and liable for the same.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: The Income Tax Department retains the authority to pursue its investigation or inquiry or scrutiny, as deemed necessary even after the amalgamation among the Respondent companies.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XI. The Transferee Company is directed to comply with the provisions of Section 233(11) of the Companies Act, 2013, in respect of payment of the requisite fees, if any, consequent upon the revision of its authorised share capital.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XII. That any person interested shall be at liberty to apply to this Tribunal in the above matter for any directions that may be necessary.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XIII. It is hereby clarified that the present Order shall not entitle the Transferee Company to any exemption from the requirement of obtaining any license/ permit/ registration/ quota/ clearance/ concession or grant that may be required by them from the Central Government/State Government/ Local Authority/Sectoral Regulator, or any other authority constituted under any law for the time being in force.

sentences.label: Facts
sentences.text: XIV. The Respondent Companies shall, within 30 days after the date of receipt of this order, cause a certified copy of this order to be delivered to the Registrar of Companies in Form INC 28.

paragraphs.text: The Company Petition 238/ND/2024 for Scheme of Amalgamation under section 233 (5) stands disposed of, in terms of above order. Sd/- Sd/- (REENA SINHA PURI) (ASHOK KUMAR BHARDWAJ) MEMBER (T) MEMBER (J)

sentences.text: The Company Petition 238/ND/2024 for Scheme of Amalgamation under section 233 (5) stands disposed of, in terms of above order.
